notes: Opportunity: openings:plaintiff. Inspected get_case and list_evidence; record contains only five initial case-packet text files and no prior filings/exhibits/submitted evidence. Read all five evidence files. ev_d15f364ccbef_market-page preserves official rule text: resolves Yes if Clavicular announces he and a partner are expecting a baby through pregnancy between market creation and Dec. 31, 2026 11:59 PM ET; non-credible announcements such as jokes do not suffice; statements from Clavicular/representatives are resolution source, with definitive consensus of credible media reporting possible. It also preserves final Yes state and API fields showing created 2026-04-20, closed/resolved 2026-05-01, proposed/disputed sequences. ev_a72d9da121a5_official-source-record and ev_f8cab9c3c684_primary-evidence state no original Clavicular/representative statement, stream, VOD, or transcript is preserved; they identify likely leads: Kick/VOD, Domahhhh X thread 2049886900513657218, JexTrade Apr. 29 post, @holy_moses7 posts, and viral Kick Champ post. ev_795651076696_secondary-reporting contains Forbes reporting favorable to plaintiff: disputed market, large volume, and argument over what counts as credible pregnancy announcement from a streamer who told his audience he had gotten a girl pregnant; but also reports market description and platform clarification said No. The same file contains adverse reports from Times of India, Hindustan Times, and Express Tribune: no confirmed pregnancy/no confirmed girlfriend/no medical update/no video confirmation; remarks about wanting children/pregnancy contest could be stunt/joke rather than existing partner pregnancy. ev_045c38eca93e_unresolved-record-gaps explicitly flags missing original source, missing platform clarification, missing UMA records, undefined credible, and secondary conflict. Native web search performed for source map only: queries included Clavicular pregnancy Polymarket Forbes April 30 2026, exact phrase 'I will be a dad in the next few months', and Domahhhh X status ID. Results confirm leads in packet: Forbes, Hindustan Times, Times of India, Domahhhh X thread, analytics/mirrors, Express Tribune, Popular Information, YouTube/Instagram/Facebook media. Search snippets are not record evidence and will not be cited as proof in opening. Opening theory: plaintiff should frame final Yes state plus Forbes direct-reporting as supporting probable qualifying announcement, while candidly identifying the primary-source gap and explaining that later evidence should decide whether the statement was a credible direct pregnancy announcement or only a non-credible contest/joke/rumor. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md with source map, search log, extraction tasks, adverse checks, and unresolved gaps.

Defendant opening work notes for arb-ex09-20260630062717.

Objective: file defendant opening statement on proposition: between market creation on 2026-04-20 and 2026-12-31 11:59 PM ET, Clavicular made a credible announcement that he and a partner were expecting a baby through pregnancy. Standard: preponderance.

Decisive elements: announcement by Clavicular/representative; he and a partner; expecting a baby through pregnancy; credible; within window. Defense theory: plaintiff must prove a credible announcement of an existing pregnancy with a partner, not a desire to have children, a hypothetical contest, internet rumor, trader opinion, or a platform final state without the source chain.

AAR record checked: get_case and list_evidence show five initial case-packet files and no newly submitted evidence. Plaintiff opening is in record. Read all five packet files using read_evidence_range.

Packet analysis: market-page proves rule text and final Yes state, including two proposal/dispute sequences, but not the underlying pregnancy announcement. official-source-record states no primary Clavicular/representative statement is locally preserved and distinguishes reported child/contest remarks from viral girlfriend-pregnant claims. primary-evidence says preserved primary/platform evidence proves contract wording and final Yes state but does not prove qualifying announcement. secondary-reporting contains Forbes helpful to plaintiff, but adverse Times of India says no proof/no confirmed girlfriend/no medical update/no video or Clavicular statement confirmed pregnancy; Hindustan Times and Express Tribune frame the remarks as a pregnancy-contest/stunt and quote desire/future-father language, not an existing partner pregnancy. unresolved-record-gaps identifies missing original clip/VOD/transcript, Polymarket clarification, UMA records, and conflict between Forbes and adverse reporting.

Native investigation before opening: searched web for (1) Clavicular pregnancy in 2026 Polymarket Forbes April 30 2026, (2) Clavicular pregnancy contest I will be a dad in next few months, (3) site:x.com domahhhh 2049886900513657218 Clavicular pregnancy. Results found Forbes, Hindustan Times, Times of India, Tribune, Polymarket page, Domahhhh X status, and Polymarket announcement X post. Search result for Domahhhh thread indicates an ambiguous claimed/joked statement about getting a girl pregnant within 10 days; must capture original thread/archive before relying in later argument. Adverse public results align with packet: contest/intent statements and unconfirmed rumor are not equivalent to a credible existing-pregnancy announcement.

Planned later evidence targets: Forbes and embedded source chain; Domahhhh X thread status 2049886900513657218; any original Kick/VOD/clip/transcript; adverse Times of India/Hindustan Times/Express Tribune captures; Polymarket clarification; Gamma/UMA records. No evidence submission allowed in opening, so outside materials will not be cited as admitted support in opening except as source targets or search-path context.

Self-audit: current admitted record does not establish the proposition by preponderance. It shows final market Yes and one secondary article's characterization, but directly flags missing primary evidence and significant conflicting reporting. The defense opening should concede what the packet proves, then argue the burden turns on the absent source chain and credibility.

Arguments:plaintiff notes. Refreshed get_case/list_evidence: no new evidence before plaintiff submissions; after submissions, list_evidence confirms three submitted plaintiff evidence items visible and text-readable: Forbes extraction ev_2c388df14c13..., Gamma API ev_b08e7634a0e9..., TOI fact-check ev_c3000953fc81.... Source map remained: decisive elements are timing after 2026-04-20, announcement by Clavicular or representative/credible-media consensus, existing expectation of baby through pregnancy with partner, and credibility rather than joke/stunt/rumor. Native investigation: fetched Gamma API with curl -L; preserved official rule, final Yes outcome, closure/resolution fields, volume/open interest, and eventMetadata describing Yes consensus from public vows/dating-show pitch plus debunked girlfriend rumors. Fetched Forbes page with curl user-agent; full article is metered/paywalled and web.open did not return clean full body, but HTML/Next.js payload exposed metadata and relevant article fragments including disputed market volume/Yes price, the phrase fragment 'gotten a girl pregnant,' rule quote, and adverse 'market description and platform clarification say No; price says Yes; oracle after two dispute rounds has not closed gap.' Opened TOI via web fetch and extracted readable article lines stating no proof/no confirmed girlfriend/no medical update/no statement from Clavicular, viral Kick Champ post, and pregnancy contest quote. Evidence submitted and accepted before reliance. Adverse analysis: TOI is strongest defense evidence and Gamma metadata is mixed because it supports final Yes/imminent fatherhood consensus but not a preserved existing partner-pregnancy statement. Forbes extraction is strongest plaintiff support but limited because no original clip/transcript captured. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md. Planned argument: preponderance from final Yes API plus rule allowing credible media consensus plus Forbes characterization; candidly concede gaps and argue TOI addresses a different viral rumor/contest context and does not negate the record evidence that Forbes reported a direct gotten-a-girl-pregnant basis.

Defendant merits argument notes.

Record scan: get_case/list_evidence show plaintiff added Forbes fragment evidence ev_2c388df14c13, Gamma API ev_b08e7634a0e9, and Times of India fact check ev_c3000953fc81. Read exact content for all three with read_evidence_range. Existing packet still includes market page, primary evidence, official-source record, secondary reporting, unresolved gaps.

Plaintiff evidence analysis: Forbes exhibit is source metadata plus fragments from a metered HTML/Next.js payload, not a full article or original statement. Its key phrase is a fragment saying 'gotten a girl pregnant' followed by the rule excluding non-credible announcements. It does not preserve original stream/VOD/transcript or context. Gamma API is strong for rule text and final Yes platform state but weak for underlying fact; eventMetadata describes repeated public vows to become a father, fertility stack reveal, dating-show/baby-mama contest pitch, viral backlash, and debunked girlfriend-pregnancy rumors, which supports ambiguity/non-credibility rather than a qualifying source. Times of India is a full adverse extraction saying no proof, no confirmed girlfriend, no medical update, no video confirms pregnancy, and no Clavicular statement confirms it; confusion came from pregnancy contest/out-of-context clips.

Native investigation: opened Hindustan Times URL and extracted readable article lines 116-166. It reports a contest where 500 women would participate and the winner would be impregnated, quotes 'I must have children...' and 'I will be a dad in the next few months...' and frames the matter as a social-media backlash to a contest/stunt, not a confirmed partner pregnancy. Submitted as ev_519a3b6f458c_submitted-evidence-04-defendant-519a3b6f458c. Opened Express Tribune URL and extracted lines 21-47. It reports the pregnancy contest, future fatherhood desire, possible joke/shock content, and no clarification whether genuine/satirical. Submitted as ev_115037a41d09_submitted-evidence-05-defendant-115037a41d09. Tried opening x.com/domahhhh/status/2049886900513657218; web open returned zero lines. Tried fxtwitter/vxtwitter variants without usable content; search snippets are not evidence and will not be cited as record support.

Argument theory: The proposition requires a credible announcement that Clavicular and a partner are expecting a baby through pregnancy. Record proves rule and final Yes state; it does not prove the qualifying statement. The admitted sources that describe actual words consistently show desire/future fatherhood/pregnancy-contest language and the fact check says no confirmation. Plaintiff relies on one incomplete Forbes fragment and platform/oracle result; preponderance should fail because the source chain is missing and credible-source conflict leans against a qualifying announcement.

Unresolved gaps: original VOD/clip/transcript; Forbes full text/source chain; Polymarket clarification said to say No; UMA records; accessible Domahhhh thread; any later direct Clavicular/representative clarification. Self-audit: no primary statement found or admitted; defense should be explicit that absence plus admitted adverse reporting defeats plaintiff burden, not that a primary source impossible exists.

Rebuttal:plaintiff notes. Refreshed get_case/list_evidence. Defense added two adverse secondary articles: Hindustan Times ev_519a3b6f458c... and Express Tribune ev_115037a41d09..., both readable. They report a pregnancy contest / desire to become a father soon / possible joke or shock content, and neither preserves original livestream video or complete transcript. Defense argument says Forbes extraction is fragmentary, Gamma API is only market metadata, TOI/HT/Tribune preserve actual reported words, and plaintiff lacks original VOD/source chain. Analysis: concede contest/future-fatherhood remarks alone would not satisfy the rule, but argue those sources address a separate/adjacent controversy and do not negate Forbes's market-specific report that the dispute involved Clavicular telling his audience he had gotten a girl pregnant. Defense treats the missing VOD as dispositive despite rule allowing credible media consensus. Platform clarification/No and dispute rounds prove controversy, not factual falsity; final Yes after dispute remains probative. No new evidence submitted in rebuttal because additional searches would likely duplicate already-admitted secondary sources and the original VOD remains unavailable in this turn. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.

Defendant surrebuttal notes.

Record scan: get_case/list_evidence show no new evidence submitted in plaintiff rebuttal. Evidence list unchanged after defendant argument: case-packet files, plaintiff Forbes/Gamma/Times of India submissions, and defense Hindustan Times/Express Tribune submissions. Plaintiff rebuttal's strongest point is conceptual: it argues defense articles concern contest/future-fatherhood remarks, while Forbes concerns a separate direct 'gotten a girl pregnant' statement.

Analysis: That 'separate category' is not preserved as a source in the record. The record has an incomplete Forbes fragment, not the underlying Clavicular statement, not a full article body, not a VOD/transcript, not a representative statement, not the platform clarification, and not UMA source records. Plaintiff bears the burden to prove that separate qualifying statement; defense does not need to disprove every possible unpreserved statement. The rule's allowance for credible-media consensus requires something like a definitive consensus; here admitted media conflict, and the fuller-context admitted sources with actual reported words all point to non-qualifying contest/future fatherhood remarks plus Times of India no-confirmation findings.

No new native search was needed because rebuttal introduced no new source, quote, URL, or evidence ID; it only reweighed the existing record. No additional evidence submitted in surrebuttal. Filing should directly answer plaintiff's 'not the same event' argument and reiterate that final Yes/platform resolution is not proof of source facts, especially where Forbes itself notes market description/platform clarification said No and Gamma context references debunked rumors.

Closing:plaintiff notes. Final get_case/list_evidence scan completed. Visible record: five case-packet files; plaintiff submitted Forbes extraction ev_2c388df14c13, Gamma API ev_b08e7634a0e9, TOI adverse fact-check ev_c3000953fc81; defendant submitted Hindustan Times ev_519a3b6f458c and Express Tribune ev_115037a41d09. Defendant surrebuttal says plaintiff has not proven a separate direct qualifying statement; media consensus is conflicted; actual preserved words are non-qualifying contest/future-fatherhood words; final Yes is not independent proof. Closing strategy: be candid that missing original VOD/full transcript/full Forbes body is the main gap and that plaintiff loses if council treats primary-source preservation as necessary. Argue contract allows credible media reporting, so missing VOD affects weight not legal sufficiency; Forbes is the only market-specific credible outlet source and says the Polymarket dispute involved a streamer who told his audience he had gotten a girl pregnant; Gamma API gives final Yes under the rule; defense sources show non-qualifying adjacent contest remarks but do not disprove the market-specific Forbes-reported statement. Journal updated at /aar-codex/aar-work-notes.md.

Defendant closing notes.

Final record scan: wait_for_opportunity returned defendant closing and plaintiff closing. get_case/list_evidence performed. No new evidence since surrebuttal. Evidence remains five case-packet items plus plaintiff Forbes/Gamma/Times of India submissions and defense Hindustan Times/Express Tribune submissions.

Plaintiff closing: concedes missing VOD is important but argues not fatal under media-consensus clause; relies on Forbes fragment plus final Yes platform state; says defense sources address an adjacent contest/backlash narrative rather than the separate Forbes-reported statement.

Closing synthesis: The decision should turn on admitted proof, not possible missing proof. Plaintiff's strongest point would be persuasive if Forbes preserved the source chain or if multiple credible sources converged on an actual Clavicular statement that he and a partner were expecting a baby. The record instead has one partial Forbes extraction, a final market state, and a Gamma context field that describes vows/fertility stack/contest pitch/debunked rumors. Against that are packet admissions that primary evidence proves only rule/final state, unresolved-gaps identifying absent VOD/transcript/clarification/UMA records, TOI no-confirmation findings, and HT/Tribune actual reported words showing non-qualifying future/contest/shock-content context. Media-consensus clause cannot be satisfied by one incomplete fragment in a conflicted record. Closing should ask council to find proposition false because preponderance is not met.
